required.title: [Materials] Wood glue, finishing nails, wood screws (assorted sizes), sandpaper (80, 120, 220 grit)

competence_markers.description: The student can measure and mark to within 1/8 inch accuracy on wood
The student can cut on a marked line with a hand saw, maintaining a reasonably straight and square cut
The student understands the difference between nails, screws, and glue and can explain when each is appropriate
The student has completed at least two functional projects using butt joints or lap joints
The student sands and finishes their projects — projects are not done when assembled, they are done when finished
The student can identify grain direction in a piece of wood and explain why it matters
The student narrates what worked, what failed, and what they would do differently on each project

content.why_this_matters:
Wood is the first material most human beings ever built with. Before metal, before concrete, before plastic — there was wood. People carved it, split it, joined it, and built shelters, boats, tools, furniture, and bridges from it. They still do.

Wood is the most forgiving building material you will work with. If you cut it wrong, you can often fix it. If a joint does not fit, you can adjust it. If a project fails, the material is inexpensive enough that you can start over without heartbreak. But wood is also honest — it shows you exactly what you did. A crooked cut is visible. A gap in a joint is visible. A surface that was not sanded is obvious to the touch. You cannot hide your work. This is what makes wood an ideal teacher.

In this packet, you will measure, mark, cut, fasten, and finish real wood to make real things. Not models. Not crafts. Things that work — a birdhouse that birds can use, a shelf that holds books, a box that stores tools. You will learn that where you cut matters, that how you join pieces matters, and that the difference between something that works and something that falls apart is attention to detail.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Measuring and marking** — transferring a dimension from plan to wood accurately
2. **Cutting to a line** — using a hand saw to cut where you intend to cut
3. **Fastening knowledge** — understanding nails, screws, and glue and when to use each
4. **Simple joinery** — butt joints and lap joints that hold
5. **Project completion** — seeing a project through from plan to finished, functional object
6. **Material awareness** — understanding that wood has grain, that grain direction matters, and that different woods behave differently
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Wood Is Not All the Same

Pick up two different pieces of wood — a piece of pine and a piece of oak, if you can find both. Immediately you notice differences. The pine is lighter. The oak is heavier. The pine is softer — you can dent it with your thumbnail. The oak resists.

This is your first lesson: wood has properties that matter for what you build.

**Softwoods** (pine, spruce, cedar, fir) come from coniferous trees — trees with needles. They grow fast, which makes them less dense. They are lighter, easier to cut and nail, and less expensive. Most construction lumber is softwood. When you build a wall frame, a deck, or a simple shelf, you are usually using softwood.

**Hardwoods** (oak, maple, walnut, cherry, ash) come from deciduous trees — trees with broad leaves. They grow slowly, which makes them denser and harder. They are heavier, more difficult to cut and shape, more expensive, and more beautiful. Fine furniture, cutting boards, and tool handles are typically made from hardwood.

For your first projects, you will use softwood — specifically pine. It is forgiving, inexpensive, and available everywhere.

### Part 2: Grain Direction

Look at a piece of wood closely. You will see lines running through it — lighter and darker bands. This is the grain. Grain is the visible pattern of the wood fibers that make up the tree's structure.

Grain direction matters for everything you do with wood:

- **Cutting with the grain** (ripping) is different from **cutting across the grain** (crosscutting). Each requires a different saw technique and, in power tool work, a different blade.
- **Splitting** happens along the grain. Wood splits easily with the grain and resists splitting across it. This is why a board nailed near its end splits — the nail forces the grain apart. (Pre-drilling a hole prevents this.)
- **Strength** runs with the grain. A board is strongest along its length and weakest across its width. A shelf bracket mounted with grain running vertically is stronger than one with grain running horizontally.

You do not need to master grain science right now. You need to notice it. Look at every piece of wood before you work with it. See the grain. Think about which direction it runs. This habit will serve you for the rest of your woodworking life.

### Part 3: Measuring and Marking

Every woodworking project begins with measurement. The board must be the right length. The holes must be in the right places. The pieces must fit together. All of this depends on accurate measuring and marking.

**The process:**
1. Read the measurement from your plan or drawing
2. Hook your tape measure over the end of the board (or butt it against a surface)
3. Find the correct measurement on the tape
4. Make a small, precise mark with a sharp pencil at exactly the right point — not a thick dot, but a thin line
5. Use your combination square to extend the mark across the face of the board — this is your cut line
6. Check your mark by measuring again from the same reference point

**Common mistakes:**
- Measuring from the wrong end of the board
- Making marks that are too thick to be precise (use a sharp pencil)
- Forgetting to account for the width of the saw cut (the kerf) when cutting multiple pieces from one board
- Measuring once and assuming it is right

### Part 4: Cutting to a Line

The most fundamental woodworking skill: putting the saw where the mark is and keeping it there.

**Hand saw technique:**
1. Secure the board — clamp it to the bench or hold it firmly in a vise with the cut line overhanging the edge
2. Position the saw on the waste side of the line — you cut the line away, not into it. The finished piece keeps the mark; the waste gets the saw kerf.
3. Start with a few short pull strokes to establish the cut. Use your thumb as a guide against the flat of the blade (not the teeth) for the first few strokes.
4. Once the cut is established, use full, smooth strokes. Let the saw do the work. Do not force it — forcing the saw causes it to wander off the line.
5. As you near the end of the cut, support the waste piece with your free hand so it does not break off and tear the grain.

Practice this on scrap wood. Many times. Before you start a project, make at least ten practice cuts on scrap and evaluate each one. Did the saw follow the line? Is the cut square (perpendicular to the face of the board)?

### Part 5: Fasteners — Nails, Screws, and Glue

There are three basic ways to hold pieces of wood together. Each works differently, and each is the right choice in different situations.

**Nails** hold by friction. The nail shaft is wedged into the wood fibers, and the friction of the fibers gripping the shaft holds the joint together. Nails are fast to drive and inexpensive. They resist pulling straight out reasonably well but resist side-to-side forces poorly. Use nails for rough construction, temporary assembly, and situations where speed matters more than strength.

**Screws** hold by threading into the wood. The screw threads cut into the fibers and pull the pieces together mechanically. Screws are much stronger than nails — especially in resisting pull-out force. They also allow disassembly (you can unscrew a screw and take the joint apart). Use screws when you need holding strength, when you might need to disassemble later, or when the joint will bear weight or stress.

**Glue** holds by bonding the wood surfaces together chemically. Modern wood glue (PVA — polyvinyl acetate, the yellow glue) creates a bond that is actually stronger than the wood itself. A properly glued joint will break in the wood, not at the glue line. But glue requires flat, clean surfaces pressed tightly together while the glue dries. This means clamps — and patience.

For your first projects, you will use a combination. Glue for strength. Nails or screws for holding the pieces in position while the glue dries and for additional mechanical strength.

### Part 6: Simple Joints

A joint is where two pieces of wood meet. The quality of the joint determines the quality of the project.

**Butt joint:** The simplest joint — the end of one board pressed against the face or edge of another. Held together with nails, screws, or glue (usually a combination). A butt joint is not strong on its own — it relies entirely on the fasteners. But it is fast, simple, and adequate for many projects.

**Lap joint:** One piece overlaps another, and the overlapping area is fastened. A lap joint has more surface area than a butt joint, which means glue has more to grip, and the joint is stronger. A half-lap joint — where each piece is cut to half its thickness so they fit flush when overlapped — is stronger still.

For Stretching Branches, butt joints and simple lap joints are sufficient. You will develop more sophisticated joinery in Weathering Storms.

### Part 7: Finishing

A finished project is not just assembled — it is smoothed, protected, and completed.

**Sanding:** Start with coarse sandpaper (80 grit) to remove rough surfaces and tool marks. Progress to medium (120 grit) for a smoother finish. Finish with fine (220 grit) for a surface that is smooth to the touch. Always sand with the grain — sanding across the grain leaves scratches that show under finish.

**Finishing options for Stretching Branches projects:**
- Paint — covers imperfections, protects the wood, adds color
- Clear finish (polyurethane or Danish oil) — shows the natural wood grain while protecting the surface
- Beeswax — a simple, non-toxic finish for items that will be handled frequently

Finishing teaches patience. Each coat must dry before the next is applied. Rushing produces a poor finish that looks worse than no finish at all.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Measurement and Marking
**Developing well:** The student measures independently, marks with a sharp pencil, and extends cut lines with a square. Measurements are within 1/8 inch consistently.

**Developing slowly:** The student needs help reading the tape measure, makes thick or imprecise marks, or forgets to mark before cutting.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student comfortable with fractions? If not, coordinate with their numeracy work (1.5.1-A) and use measurement practice as a concrete application.

### 2. Cutting
**Developing well:** The saw follows the line. The student uses proper technique (smooth strokes, lets the saw work, supports the waste piece). Cuts are reasonably straight and square.

**Developing slowly:** The saw wanders off the line, the student forces the saw, or cuts are significantly out of square.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the saw sharp? A dull saw is nearly impossible for a child to use well. Is the workpiece properly clamped? A workpiece that moves during cutting makes accurate cutting impossible.

### 3. Project Completion
**Developing well:** The student completes projects from start to finish: planning, measuring, cutting, assembling, sanding, finishing. They narrate what worked and what they would change.

**Developing slowly:** The student loses interest partway through, skips finishing, or cannot identify what went wrong when the project has problems.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the project appropriately sized? A project that takes more than 3-4 sessions for a young child is too long. Break larger projects into milestones so the student experiences completion at each stage.
